coredata.dc:title: ZnO nanosheets encapsulating graphene quantum dots with enhanced performance for dye-sensitized solar cell
coredata.dc:creator: Mati Ur Rahman,
Xie, Fengyan
Li, Yafeng
Wei, Mingdeng
coredata.dc:description: A composite of ZnO nanosheets (ZNSs)/graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was synthesized via a facile synthetic route, and used as an electrode for the dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). The ZNSs/GQDs photoanode exhibited a relatively high photocurrent of 12.2mAcm−2 and the power conversion efficiency of 5.27% can be achieved, which is 41% higher than that of the pristine sample. The improvement might be ascribed to the superior charge mobility of the ZNSs/GQDs composite. It was also found that the incorporation of the GQDs on surface of ZNSs can lead to the increase of the surface area, dye adsorption capability, light harvesting and electron transport rate, resulting in the enhanced performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZNSs/GQDs
Surface modification
Dye-sensitized solar cell
Photovoltaic performance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Materials characterization and photovoltaic performance mensuration

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) X-ray diffraction patterns of ZNSs, (b) XPS spectra of ZNSs and ZNSs/GQDs: (c) high-resolution spectra of Zn2p, and (d) UV–visible absorption spectra.
SEM images of (a) ZNSs, (b) ZNSs/GQDs, (c-d) TEM images of ZNSs/GQDs, (e) STEM image and (f-h) elemental mapping of ZNSs/GQDs, respectively.
Photocurrent J-V curves of the DSSC with the films of ZNSs and ZNSs/GQDs.
IPCE spectra of the ZNSs and ZNSs/GQDs based DSSCs.
EIS spectra of the cells composed of ZNSs and ZNSs/GQDs electrodes.
Photovoltaic performance for the cells composed of ZNSs and ZNSs/GQDs photoanodes.
EIS parameters of DSSCs based on ZNSs and ZNSs/GQDs working electrodes.

head.ce:title: ZnO nanosheets encapsulating graphene quantum dots with enhanced performance for dye-sensitized solar cell

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, Fujian 350002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Energy Materials, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, Fujian 350002, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, Fujian 350002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A composite of ZnO nanosheets (ZNSs)/graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was synthesized via a facile synthetic route, and used as an electrode for the dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). The ZNSs/GQDs photoanode exhibited a relatively high photocurrent of 12.2mAcm−2 and the power conversion efficiency of 5.27% can be achieved, which is 41% higher than that of the pristine sample. The improvement might be ascribed to the superior charge mobility of the ZNSs/GQDs composite. It was also found that the incorporation of the GQDs on surface of ZNSs can lead to the increase of the surface area, dye adsorption capability, light harvesting and electron transport rate, resulting in the enhanced performance.

ZNSs/GQDSs with high surface area, excellent dye adsorption and superior charge mobility leading to a high photocurrent and power conversion efficiency.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Increasing fossil fuel combustion caused an adverse impact on global environment, so for controlling environmental pollution and energy crisis has made a prodigious challenge for modern world [1,2]. Hence more effort has been focused on solar energy driven, to optimize fossil fuels depletion and desirable energy conversion [3,4]. For the conversion of solar energy directly into electricity, dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) have aroused tremendous interest from last two decades as a new energy device. Such a device can be fabricated within relatively short time and is considered benign for the environment and the cheap alternative to silicon-based solar cells [5]. The working electrode is considered as the core component of DSSCs. So far, the transportation of electron to wide bandgap semiconductors from colored dyes is technologically important in the photoelectrochemical energy conversion. Among the wide bandgap semiconductors, titanium dioxide (TiO2) is most extensively studied as a photoanode since 1991 [6]. However, the power conversion efficiency is still not >15% [7]. On the other hand, zinc oxide (ZnO) is most commonly used as a photoanode in DSSCs except for TiO2, owing to its similar band gape energy (Eg), i.e. 3.2eV and 3.3eV compared with TiO2 [8,9] but has excellent photosensitivity, thermal stability, electron mobility [10–12], electron diffusivity, large excitation binding energy and richer in diverse morphologies in comparison to any known materials [13]. Ideal semiconductor for working electrode should have photo-activity, benign for environment, photo-stability, reasonable low cost, non-toxicity, well crystallinity and large surface area [14,15]. ZnO is the one of semiconductor which can fulfill the entire aforementioned characteristic. The main problem in ZnO working electrode is the recombination of photo-generated electrons which can be effectively control by the direct and rapid electron transport channel construction. Therefore, one-dimensional ZnO materials with various morphologies are commonly used in (DSSCs), but their power energy conversion efficiencies are still poor due to their small surface area and incapability of dye adsorption. Two-dimensional ZNSs not only have the rapid and direct electron transport channels but also possess the large surface area and good dye adsorption capability [16]. In fact, a lot of researches have done over the synthesis of nanocomposite and heterojunction of ZnO nanomaterials with carbon nanotubes (CNTs), revealing that it can increase the simplex ZnO and CNTs materials pristine properties [17]. Graphene possesses identical physical characteristic of large surface area and low production cost in comparison with CNTs [18]. Therefore, it is highly expected as an alternate low cost material to CNTs in nanocomposites [19]. For the DSSCs performance improvement different efficient approach has been adopted like to configure CoS sheets-coupled graphene quantum dots (GQDs) architectures as counter electrode etc. [20]. Additionally, GQDs is suitable for optoelectronic device and meritorious in comparison to the other QDs like no defect on surface, toxicity, large surface area, having capability of functionalization on the edge of GQDs and high thermal stability [21,22]. What's more, the edge-functionalization of GQDs and unique structure of sp2 carbon domain facilitate spontaneously ways to surface passivation for DSSCs [23,24].

In the present work, 2D ZNSs were synthesized which could provide high surface area for dye adsorption. On the other hand, GQDs were deposited on the surface of ZNSs in order to increase adsorption of dye molecular. It was found that the ZNSs/GQDs photoanode exhibited an increment in light harvest efficiency, dye-adsorption capability, and a decrease in photo-generated charge recombination rate.

All materials of analytical grade reagents were used in this study without further purification and purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, AR 99%) and urea (CO(NH2)2, AR 99%) were used as a precursor of ZnO and surfactant respectively.

ZNS and ZNS/GQDs were fabricated via a facile synthesis route. In a typical synthesis, 4mM of zinc nitrate hexahydrate and 16mM urea were dissolved in 250mL deionized water under vigorous stirring. After 0.5h stirring the mixture solution was introduced to a round-bottom flask and reflux for 1h at 90°C in oil bath. Then, the above solution was subjected to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and the autoclave was heated to 160°C for 12h. Afterword, the resulted precipitate was collected by filtration, thoroughly washed with deionized water and dried under vacuum at 80°C. Finally, the dried powder was calcined at 400°C for 3.5h to obtain ZNS sample. And for the synthesis of ZNS/GQDs nanocomposite the same aforementioned procedure was adopted. Briefly, the GQDs obtained from acid vapor cutting strategy [25] of MOF-derived carbon (ZIF-8) were added at the start of ZNS growth solution.

A number of various pastes were prepared according to previously reported literature study [26]. Typically, ZNSs, ZNSs/GQDs, α-terpineol and ethyl cellulose (5, 10% by weight) were homogeneously mixed in absolute ethanol through Ultraturrax T25 homogenizer and concentrated up to 20wt% via rotary evaporator. Photoanodes were constructed on FTO glass substrate (sheet resistance 8Ω sq.−1, Pilkington) by screen printing method, and then sintered at 450°C for 0.5h. Then the as prepared working electrodes were submerged for 2h at 25°C in 0.5mMN719 dye solution in isopropyl alcohol (1:1v/v) to absorb dye completely, rinsed and followed by nitrogen gas drying. Counter electrodes were prepared by drop coating of chloroplatinic acid solution on FTO substrate and then heated for 0.5h at 400°C. The polyethylene spacer was adjusted between two electrodes and the redox electrolyte composed of 0.05M I2, 0.1M LiI, 0.6M 1, 2-dimethyl-3-propylimidazolium iodide, and 1.0M 4-ter-butylpyridine, was injected between the gap of electrodes and polyethylene spacer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials characterization and photovoltaic performance mensuration
ce:section.ce:para: The crystallographic elucidation of the materials was studied from X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern conducted through a Rigaku Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation source (λ=1.542Å) and 2θ/min scanning rate from 5 to 70° operating at 40mA, 40kV current, and accelerating voltage. Morphologically the materials were analyzed via (Hitachi S4800 SEM) scanning electron microscope. The ASAP 2020 surface analyzer (Micromeritics, Norcross, GA, USA) were used for textural information like pore size and surface area through N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm.

Thickness of the film was measured with (Accretech surfcom 130A) profilometer. The spectroscopic observations were obtained using RENISHAW inVia Raman spectroscope in ultraviolet light with high-resolution gratins, 3000 to 100cm−1 spectra were recorded and Shimadzu UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer. XPS (X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy) experiments were conducted on an ESCALAB 250 photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific) with Al Kα monochromatic X-ray radiation (hν =1486.60eV). The binding energy of the elements was fixed to that for carbon (284.60eV). The photovoltaic performance was tested under simulated solar light AM1.5G illumination with Keithley 2400 (Peccell, with AM 1.5 filter and 1000W Xe lamp) source meter in ambient atmosphere. The PEC S-20 (Peccell, Technology Co, Ltd) multimeter was used for the conduction of incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPEC) spectra. EIS (electrochemical impedance spectroscopy) was observed using an IM6 (Zahner) electrochemical workstation equipped with −0.7V bias potential and 80mHz–3kHz range in dark condition. The dye loading of the photoanodes has been determined photometrically. The ZNSs and ZNSs/GQDs photoanodes loaded with N719 dyes were immersed in 10mM NaOH at 30°C for 12h. The most intense band intensity in the region of 550 to 515nm of the adsorption spectrum was used to calibrate the dye loading.

The XRD patterns of ZNSs and ZNSs/GQDS are depicted in Fig. 1A. All of the diffraction peaks are intense, well defined and can be indexed to the pure hexagonal wurtzite phase of ZnO (JCPDS# 01-075-0576), indicating that the fabricated materials have high crystallinity and there is no detectable impurity. Because amount of GQDs is small, it cannot be detected by XRD measurement. The Fig. 1B shows XPS survey spectra of the ZNSs and ZNSs/GQDs and as labeled in aforementioned figure all of the peaks indexing to Zn, O and C without any impurities. The more distinctive peaks centralized at 1021, 1044, 475, 531, 284, 98, 140 and 10eV reveal the binding energy of Zn2P3/2, Zn2P1/2, ZnLMM1, O1s, C1s, Zn3d, Zn3s and Zn3p, respectively. Fig. 1C displays the high-resolution XPS spectra of ZNSs and ZNSs/GQDs, the bands centered at 1044.17 and 1021.07eV can respectively be ascribed to Zn2p1/2 and Zn2p3/2 (Zn2+ oxidation) states [27]. The ZNSs and ZNSs/GQDs samples optical absorption spectra were recorded in the UV–Vis range at room temperature and the results are manifested in Fig. 1(d). The UV–Vis spectra well defined absorption edge at round about 400nm, indicating the characteristics of crystalline ZNSs. However, the intensity of their absorption tail shifted to the higher wavelength after the incorporation of GQDs, which is the clearly evidence of presence of GQDs on the surface of ZNSs and improve the response to incident light. Furthermore, the color of ZNSs sample changes from white to ash white, displaying the dispersive nature of the GODs.

The structural, size and elemental distributions on the surface of ZNSs were elucidated via scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM). The SEM images in Fig. 2(a–b) clearly indicate that as prepared material and after the incorporation of quantum dots almost maintain distinctive porous nanosheets morphology with rugged surface. Furthermore, large-scale morphological homogeneity through whole sample can also be observed from these images. Fig. 2(c–d) displays the TEM images of ZNSs/GQDs, which further confirm the sheet-like structure with a large number of voids, homogenous distribution of quantum dots (lower inset of Fig. 2d) and single crystalline nature (upper inset of Fig. 2d).

As shown in Fig. 2d, the ZNSs surface can be clearly observed, which was well garnished by GQDs evenly and sparsely. What's more, the corresponding TEM image and elemental mapping measurement (Fig. 2e–h) of the respective element distribution, verified the existence and uniformity of oxygen, zinc and carbon in the whole sample. One of the important factor that significantly affect the DSSCs performance is dye loading capability which are mainly concern with pore size distribution and specific surface area of the materials used as a dye sensitizer working electrode. Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm was measured and used to study the specific surface area and pore size distribution of ZNSs and ZNSs/GQDs. The results manifested in Fig. S1(a–b) confirm that the surface area greatly altered after the incorporation of GQDs which are calculated to be 82 and 108 m2 g−1, respectively. On the other hand, Raman spectroscopy is an effective technique, provides subtle phase information, crystalline properties and structural defects. The Raman spectra of ZNSs and ZNSs/GQDs samples depicted in Fig. S2 display four well defined peaks, located at about 207, 327, 100 and 439cm−1 respectively are attributed to the multiphonon process or second order modes, E2L and E2H mode of hexagonal wurtizite type zinc oxide structure [28–30]. The E2H Raman peak belongs to oxygen sub-lattice of zinc oxide, which is slightly shift to higher wavelength, become broader and more intense after the deposition of GQDs on the surface of ZNSs. What's more, the D and G band lies at 1343 and 1590cm−1 ascribed to the presence of GQDs.

J-V characteristics of ZNSs and ZNSs/GQDs based DSSCs were investigated and results are displayed in Fig. 3 , and the related photovoltaic parameters are summarized in Table 1 . It can be realized that the device based on ZNSs featured a short-circuit current (Jsc) of 9.16mAcm−2, an open-circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.63V and an overall photo-conversion efficiency of 3.73%.

Notably, the decoration of GQDs layer over ZNSs significantly boosted the device performance. As listed in Table 1, after the incorporation of GQDs, the dye loading amount increased from 1.18 to 1.39 10−7 molcm−2, and Jsc, Voc and η were increased to 12.2mAcm−2, 0.68V and 5.27%, respectively, which was 41% increment in comparison to the reference ZNSs based device. What's more, the causes for significant improvement of Jsc can be evidenced by the IPCE (incident photo-to-current conversion efficiency). As displayed in Fig. 4 , the device based on ZNSs decorated with GQDs almost exhibits much higher IPCE in the range of 300 to 800nm wavelength than pure ZNSs based one.

The charge injection (ηinj), charge collection (ηc.coll) light-harvesting (LHE), and dye regeneration quantum efficiency (ηdre) are the main factors for IPCE, as displayed in Eq. (1) [31].
(1)
IPCE =
LHEη dre
η inj
η
c . coll

The combination of the criterion iodine based electrolyte and N719 dye are applied, so ηdre and ηinj are regarded to be unity [31]. The GQDs layer is a thin layer and the overall electrode thickness is almost similar in the two devices, so the LHE are changed to some extent by the incorporation of GQDs. The significantly boosted Jsc and η could be partly due to improved LHE induced by the absorption of light and sensitization of ZnO through GQDs. But it is not the main factor and evidence for the IPCE increment. In consequence of that, the increment in Jsc and/or IPCE equate with ηc.coll, which further relates to the ratio between the charge transfer resistance (Rct1) and the recombination resistance (Rct2) as displayed in Eq. (2) [31,32].
(2)
η
c . coll
= 1 −
R
ct 1
R
ct 2
whereas Rct1 is the charge transport resistance counter balance between the ZNSs transparent electrode and the electron transport resistance beyond the ZNSs/FTO interface, while the Rct2 is seen as to the average of two electron loss mechanism beyond electrolyte/FTO and ZNSs-dye/electrolyte interfaces. Both cells exhibit identical Rct1 but different Rct2 can be ascribed to the similar condition of counter electrode and the surface modification in photoanode material. We know from Eq. (2) that reduction in the ratio between Rct1 and Rct2 leads to the increase in ηc.coll and, in turn, resulted in improvement in the overall photo conversion efficiency of the device.

To prove this hypothesis, the EIS analysis was conducted and the Nyquist curves of the devices are shown in Fig. 5 , while the results are summarized in Table 2 . As from the EIS spectra featuring two (small and large) semicircles, located in high and middle frequency. The former small semicircle can be caused by the counter-electrode/electrolyte interface charge transfer resistance, and the latter large semicircle corresponds to the charge transfer process in working electrode. From the closer examination of the EIS analysis, the device based on ZNSs/GQDs shows higher Rct2 compared to ZNSs based device, which leads to a lowering charge recombination process or decrease in the ratio between Rct1 and Rct2, leading to overall charge-collection, Jsc enhancement and, ultimately, of the device efficiency.

In summary, ZNSs, GQDs and the incorporation of GQDs on the surface of ZNSs were reported via a simple synthetic route and then were used as electrodes for DSSCs. It was found that the cell composed of GQDS on the surface ZNSs exhibited the best efficiency of 5.27% which is 41% enhancement comparable to that of ZNSs-based device; this might be contributed to the facts that the charge collection at the photoanode can be improved through the deposition of the GQDs on the surface of ZNSs, lowering the loss of photo-excited electron, increment in specific surface area and dye loading capability. Thus, it can be concluded that our systematic work and the facile synthetic approach will exhibit a superior route for the synthesis of new materials and the enhancement for the performance of the DSSCs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC 91433104, U1505241).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2019.03.074.
